coredata.dc:title: Electrocatalytic degradation of high concentration of hygromycin hydrochloride in mariculture wastewater by Pd NPs composite Ti electrode dispersed with 0D/1D CQDs/CNTs heterogeneous interface

coredata.dc:description: With the rapid development of mariculture, a large amount of wastewater containing high concentrations of antibiotics is continuously discharged into coastal waters, posing a serious threat to the marine ecosystem and human health. To address this problem, in this study, we successfully prepared a composite electrode of palladium/carbon quantum dots-carbon nanotubes/titanium (Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti) for the electrocatalytic degradation of high-concentration antibiotic wastewater in seawater. This was achieved through a composite modification strategy using nanomaterials combined with ultrasonication and constant-current deposition methods. The composite electrode effectively encapsulated carbon quantum dots (CQDs) inside carbon nanotubes (CNTs), constructing a highly conjugated CQDs-CNTs heterojunction. This significantly enhanced the exposure of active sites and optimized the electron transfer pathway, thereby greatly improving the catalytic performance of the electrode. The heterostructure facilitated the optimization of the electron transfer path and provided electronic support for the Pd nanoparticles, which are the main catalytic active centers. Under optimized experimental conditions, the electrode exhibited a high degradation efficiency of 90.3 % for antibiotic Oxytetracycline Hydrochloride (OTC) with low energy consumption of 0.366 kWh/m3. The study further revealed the synergistic mechanism between the cathode and anode, demonstrating that oxidation and reduction reactions worked together in the OTC degradation process. The main active species included ·OH, active chlorine species, H*, and H2O2. Additionally, the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti composite electrode could effectively reduce the toxicity of intermediate products during OTC degradation. This not only provides an efficient and environmentally friendly method for the application of electrocatalytic technology in the remediation of antibiotic water pollution but also makes a significant contribution to marine environmental protection and sustainable development.

head.ce:title: Electrocatalytic degradation of high concentration of hygromycin hydrochloride in mariculture wastewater by Pd NPs composite Ti electrode dispersed with 0D/1D CQDs/CNTs heterogeneous interface

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the rapid development of mariculture, a large amount of wastewater containing high concentrations of antibiotics is continuously discharged into coastal waters, posing a serious threat to the marine ecosystem and human health. To address this problem, in this study, we successfully prepared a composite electrode of palladium/carbon quantum dots-carbon nanotubes/titanium (Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti) for the electrocatalytic degradation of high-concentration antibiotic wastewater in seawater. This was achieved through a composite modification strategy using nanomaterials combined with ultrasonication and constant-current deposition methods. The composite electrode effectively encapsulated carbon quantum dots (CQDs) inside carbon nanotubes (CNTs), constructing a highly conjugated CQDs-CNTs heterojunction. This significantly enhanced the exposure of active sites and optimized the electron transfer pathway, thereby greatly improving the catalytic performance of the electrode. The heterostructure facilitated the optimization of the electron transfer path and provided electronic support for the Pd nanoparticles, which are the main catalytic active centers. Under optimized experimental conditions, the electrode exhibited a high degradation efficiency of 90.3 % for antibiotic Oxytetracycline Hydrochloride (OTC) with low energy consumption of 0.366 kWh/m3. The study further revealed the synergistic mechanism between the cathode and anode, demonstrating that oxidation and reduction reactions worked together in the OTC degradation process. The main active species included ·OH, active chlorine species, H*, and H2O2. Additionally, the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti composite electrode could effectively reduce the toxicity of intermediate products during OTC degradation. This not only provides an efficient and environmentally friendly method for the application of electrocatalytic technology in the remediation of antibiotic water pollution but also makes a significant contribution to marine environmental protection and sustainable development.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the global economy expands and living standards rise, there has been a significant increase in seafood consumption, driving the rapid growth of the mariculture industry [1]. This expansion, however, has led to widespread use of antibiotics, essential antimicrobial agents in aquaculture. As a result, vast amounts of wastewater from mariculture, heavily laden with antibiotics, are discharged into the environment [2,3]. It is believed that annually, hundreds of millions of tons of these potent antibiotics seep into coastal waters [4]. One commonly used antibiotic in mariculture is Oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OTC), favored for its antibacterial properties and affordability [5]. Nonetheless, antibiotics pose severe health risks to humans, including endocrine disruption, carcinogenic effects, and both chronic and acute toxicity to marine life [6]. In order to remove high concentrations of antibiotics from coastal watersheds, researchers have developed various techniques such as adsorption [7] and biological methods [8]. However, traditional pollutant degradation processes are no longer able to fulfill the effective degradation of pollutants due to defects such as secondary pollution, process complexity, and low removal efficiency [9,10]. In contrast, the electrocatalytic process has great potential for application. Since the electrocatalytic method can maintain reactive chlorinated substances (RCS) in a high-salt environment [11] and no harmful by-products are generated during the degradation process, it is essential for the protection of marine ecosystems. Nonetheless, the choice of electrode materials significantly affects the performance of electrocatalytic degradation of antibiotics, making it crucial to seek an efficient and economically stable electrode material.

In the process of electrocatalytic degradation of OTC, the choice of electrode materials is crucial. The use of bare electrodes for catalytic reactions has many limitations, such as slow electron transfer, resulting in low reaction efficiency; a high overpotential is required to drive the reaction, which not only increases energy consumption but may also place a greater burden on the electrode material itself. Therefore, selecting suitable compounds to modify the electrode surface is an important electrode synthesis strategy [12]. In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted more and more attention due to their low cost, large specific surface area, high electron mobility, and abundant active sites [13]. CQDs have unique quantum size effects and surface effects, and their smaller particle size allows electrons to complete transfer in a shorter time, thereby improving electron mobility. The larger specific surface area provides more active sites for the reaction, which is conducive to the adsorption and reaction of OTC molecules on the electrode surface. The rich active sites can be fully contacted with OTC molecules to promote the degradation reaction. It is worth noting that the composite of CQDs with other nano-carbon materials can improve the durability of CQDs and synergistically enhance the catalytic activity. On the one hand, nano-carbon materials can provide support and protection for CQDs, reduce their agglomeration and loss during the reaction, thereby improving the durability of CQDs; on the other hand, the synergy between the two can enhance the electron transport capacity and improve the catalytic activity. In order to improve the catalytic activity of CQDs, physical modifications such as electrostatic adsorption, hydrogen bond construction, and non-covalent interactions such as π-π stacking can be used to blend CQDs with other nanomaterials to form nanocomposites or hybrids. And the construction of CQDs-based composites usually only needs to be achieved by stirring or heating in one step, and no complicated reaction settings are required [14]. By stirring or heating, CQDs can be fully contacted and interacted with other nanomaterials to form stable composites. At the same time, this preparation method can also be adjusted and optimized as needed to obtain CQDs-based composites with different properties and functions.

Choosing the right materials for electrodes is vital in the electrocatalytic breakdown of OTC. Utilizing unmodified electrodes in such reactions presents multiple drawbacks, including sluggish electron transfer rates, excessive overpotential, and limited selectivity. Consequently, modifying electrode surfaces with appropriate compounds emerges as a key tactic in electrode fabrication. In recent times, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered considerable interest due to their benefits like affordability, expansive specific surface area, swift electron movement, and plentiful active sites. Remarkably, pairing CQDs with other carbon-based nanomaterials can augment the longevity of CQDs and collectively boost their catalytic efficiency. To enhance the performance of CQDs further, physical alterations such as non-covalent bonds—including electrostatic attraction, hydrogen bonding, and π-π interactions—can be utilized to merge CQDs with additional nanomaterials, creating composite or hybrid structures. Moreover, crafting CQDs-based composites typically involves a simple one-step procedure that necessitates only stirring or heating, thereby avoiding intricate reaction configurations.

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs), as a type of carbon material, are typical one-dimensional (1D) structures. They have been widely used in electrocatalysis due to their unique electronic structure with multiple electron orbitals. Additionally, the high electrical conductivity, large surface area, chemical stability, and mechanical strength of CNTs make them attractive for electrocatalytic applications [15,16]. By compositing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with CNTs (CQDs-CNTs), highly conjugated heterojunctions can be formed, improving the dispersion of CQDs and resulting in higher surface area and more active sites. Anh [17] et al. synthesized a CD/CNT composite as an effective substitute catalyst for ORR. Their findings indicated that the Tafel slope ((−60.9 mV dec-1) and durability (∼9.1 % decline over 10 h) of CD/CNT surpassed those of high-performance Pt/C catalysts. Pei [15] et al. developed three-dimensional nanocatalysts by combining multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) with easy-to-modify B- and N-doped carbon dots (CDs). These catalysts exhibit high electrical conductivity and a large specific surface area. Therefore, combining the respective properties of CQDs and CNTs can produce novel nanomaterials.

The exceptional performance of metal nanoparticles in the electrocatalytic degradation of OTCs can be attributed to several key factors. Firstly, their high specific surface area enhances catalytic efficiency by providing ample active sites for reactions. Secondly, their good stability ensures prolonged effectiveness and reliability during use. Additionally, low energy consumption makes this process more sustainable and cost-effective. Finally, the flexibility in modulation allows for tailored adjustments to optimize performance based on specific needs [18]. However, in practical applications, their tendency to agglomerate easily leads to decreased catalytic activity, thus limiting their use. Recently, it has been shown that CQDs-CNTs as carbon carriers are beneficial for the dispersion of metal particles [19–21]. Zhang [22] et al. synthesized N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)/multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) carriers for loading Pt particles through a one-pot hydrothermal treatment process with varying contents of precursors. The results demonstrated that an appropriate amount of NCQD precursor significantly promoted the dispersion of MWCNTs in solution and provided numerous oxygen-containing groups for the deposition of Pt nanoparticles, facilitating the formation of uniform Pt nanoparticles. However, transition metals such as Pt and Rh limit large-scale applications due to their low reserves and high costs. In contrast, Pd is more cost-effective among transition metals and can activate H2O and H+ due to its incompletely filled d orbitals, which generate atomic H* and capture electrons [23–25]. Furthermore, the Pd surface possesses the ability to moderately adsorb reactive molecules, facilitating the creation of “activation complexes” and thereby leading to a comparatively high level of activity.

In this study, CQDs were successfully prepared using a top-down synthesis method. These CQDs were then encapsulated in CNT tubes through heating, stirring, and ultrasonication to construct CQDs-CNTs heterostructures. The Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti bifunctional cathode was then constructed using CQDs-CNTs as the interlayer to facilitate the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC. During the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC, atomic hydrogen (H*), an important active substance, significantly enhances the degradation efficiency. In the bifunctional cathode prepared in this study, the synergistic effect of the two components in the heterostructure of CQDs-CNTs can increase the electron density on the catalyst surface, providing more electrons for the reduction of H*, thereby promoting the generation of H*. CQDs have good electron transport capacity and high specific surface area, which can provide additional electrons for CNTs, while the three-dimensional network structure of CNTs facilitates the rapid transmission and dispersion of electrons. This synergistic effect increases the electron density on the catalyst surface, creating favorable conditions for the reduction of H+. When H+ gets electrons on the catalyst surface, H* is generated, thereby improving the degradation efficiency. On the other hand, as the main catalytic activity center, the H+ adsorbed on the surface of Pd nanoparticles is quickly converted into H* after obtaining electrons, further improving the yield of H*. Pd nanoparticles have small particle size and high surface energy, which can provide rich activity check points for the adsorption and reduction of H+. When H+ is adsorbed on the surface of Pd nanoparticles, electrons can be quickly obtained and converted into H* due to the good catalytic properties of Pd. These generated H* can react with OTC molecules and promote the degradation of OTC. The bifunctional Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode's catalytic performance and energy usage in the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC were thoroughly assessed. The formation and key roles of active chlorine species and H2O2 were also illustrated. The effectiveness of the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode was evaluated in various antibiotic solutions and water environments to address practical pollution issues. Additionally, the toxicity of OTC and its byproducts during electrocatalysis was investigated. The high degradation efficiency and low reaction energy consumption make it a promising candidate for future practical applications.
All experimental materials used in this study are described in Supplementary Text S1.

The CQDs were prepared using an electrochemical etching method based on a previous study [26]. The preparation process of CQDs-CNTs complexes is specifically described in Supplementary Text S2, and a schematic diagram of the specific preparation process is shown in Scheme 1 (a).

To facilitate the subsequent electrodeposition of CQDs-CNTs, Ti sheets with smooth surfaces were roughened through anodic oxidation. A mixed solution of 1.0 mol/L Na2SO4 and 0.5 % NaF was used as the electrolyte. The titanium sheets served as the anode, while Pt sheets (10 mm × 10 mm) acted as the cathode in 100 mL of this electrolyte for the oxidation process. The oxidized Ti electrode undergoes surface etching, transforming from a smooth to a rough texture with pores. This process enhances the adhesion of CQDs-CNTs and Pd nanoparticles, thereby improving the stability and durability of the electrode. The spacing between the two electrodes was maintained at 2 cm, and the etching voltage was set to 20 V for a duration of 1 h. Throughout this process, NaF reacts with the Ti electrode's surface, forming a soluble titanium fluoride compound. As the reaction progresses, a porous structure emerges on the surface. Additionally, during anodic oxidation, NaF facilitates the growth of the oxide film by supplying essential fluoride ions. This makes the oxide film denser and more robust, thereby enhancing the stability of the Ti electrode. Subsequent experiments were carried out using the etched Ti electrodes, and the specific preparation process of the composite electrodes is described in Supplementary Text S3.

The surface micromorphology of Ti, CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrodes, and Pd/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrodes composite electrodes was examined using field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Apreo 2C, Thermo Scientific). The heterostructures of CQDs-CNTs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Apreo 2C, Thermo Scientific). The crystal structures of the composite electrodes were verified through X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance), while the surface elemental valence states of the MXene/TiO2 NAs@CC composite electrode were analyzed with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB Xi+, Thermo Fisher). Additionally, the presence of hydroxyl radicals (·OH)) in the reaction solution was detected using a RF-5301 PC fluorescence spectrophotometer from Japan. In addition, the electrochemical characterization (CV, LSV, EIS, Tafel) used in this experiment was carried out by the PARSTAT 3000A-DX electrochemical workstation. The reference electrode used in the experiment was the saturated calomel electrode. In the electrocatalytic degradation process, the prepared composite electrode was used as the cathode, and a platinum sheet with a size of 1 cm × 1 cm was used as the counter electrode. Under the action of Lei Magnetic DJH-292 double significant potentiostat, the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC was carried out in a constant current mode. The intermediates produced during the degradation process of OTC were identified using an Agilent 1206 liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry system (LC-MS) from the USA. The concentration of OTC was determined by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) as described in Supplementary Text S4. In addition, during the experiment, three parallel samples were prepared by the same method in each group of experiments, and degradation experiments were carried out to obtain experimental errors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apparent morphology analysis of Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological characteristics of CQDs-CNTs were examined utilizing transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1 (a-b) illustrate the TEM images of CQDs-CNTs, distinctly revealing that CQDs are securely anchored to the internal surface of the CNTs' tubular framework. This observation verifies the successful encapsulation of CQDs within CNTs, forming highly conjugated heterojunctions between CQDs and CNTs [12]. Additionally, to ensure that the black spots observed in the TEM images were not due to structural defects in the CNTs, we conducted a TEM test on the undoped CNTs, as shown in Fig. S1. The results indicate that the internal structure of the pure CNTs is intact and free from impurities. Thus, this further confirms the successful doping of the CQDs. The incorporation of CQDs not only exposes additional active sites but also establishes an electron transfer pathway for the CNTs. Moreover, CQDs feature exceptional edge active sites, thereby offering a greater number of active sites for catalytic reactions [15]. Subsequent scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of the Ti substrate (Fig. 1c) indicate a relatively smooth surface, which leads to a smaller specific surface area for the electrode and consequently weaker electrocatalytic performance during degradation processes. However, the etched Ti matrix (Fig. 1d) exhibits an orderly arrangement of nanotubular structures, providing a significantly larger surface area conducive to increased material loading. Fig. 1(e) presents the SEM image of the electrode following CQDs-CNTs loading, where the loaded CQDs-CNTs particles appear as flocculated aggregates with unclear visibility. In contrast, Fig. 1(f) shows the electrode after Pd loading, with crystal particles clearly visible and evenly distributed across the surface. This uniform distribution enhances the electrode's specific surface area, providing more active sites for electrocatalytic reactions. Such improvement facilitates the migration of pollutants from the solution to the electrode surface, thereby enhancing the electrode's electrocatalytic efficiency. Additionally, the mapping test image in Fig. 1(g) of the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode confirms the presence of four elements: Ti, C, O, and Pd. Notably, the elements C, O, and Pd are uniformly distributed throughout the entire electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elemental valence and crystallographic analysis of Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the crystal phase composition of the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti composite electrode, XRD analysis was performed on the composite electrode. The results are shown in Fig. 2 (a). The main diffraction peaks of Ti were observed at 2θ values of 35.0°, 39.1°, 40.0°, 51.9°, 61.7°, 71.6°, 74.9°, 76.2°, and 77.4°, corresponding to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), and (201) crystal planes of Ti, respectively. Additionally, a weaker diffraction peak at 2θ = 44.98° was detected, which can be attributed to the (101) crystal face of the graphite phase, indicating the successful loading of CQDs-CNTs onto the electrode [27–29]. However, the peak intensity is weak due to the influence of the Ti substrate and the limited amount of CQDs-CNTs on the electrode surface. Furthermore, a characteristic peak of Pd was observed at 2θ = 40.0°, corresponding to the (111) crystallographic plane of Pd, confirming the presence of Pd in the composite electrode [30]. Notably, when 2θ is 40.0°, this characteristic diffraction peak represents the overlapping peak of the (101) crystal plane of Ti and the (111) crystal plane of Pd.

To determine the surface chemical composition of the composite electrode, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyze the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode. Fig. S2 presents a full-scan XPS spectrum, revealing that the composite electrode consists primarily of Ti, C, O, and Pd elements. The narrow scan spectrum of C 1s in Fig. 2(b) can be decomposed into three peaks: C–C/C=C (284.6 eV), C-OH (285.1 eV), and –COOH (288.6 eV) [31]. The core-level hybridized carbon structures are mainly derived from CNTs, while the high content of oxygen-containing functional groups is associated with CQDs [32]. The presence of –COOH confirms the successful loading of CNTs, as carboxylated multi-walled carbon nanotubes were used in this study [26,33,34]. The O 1s spectrum in Fig. 2(c) can be deconvoluted into two characteristic peaks: one at 531.6 eV attributed to surface adsorbed oxygen (AO), and another at 530.0 eV corresponding to lattice oxygen (LO). The lower peak intensity of non-lattice oxygen indicates increased surface disorder of carbon nanotubes due to the presence of CQDs [35]. The XPS pattern of Pd 3d is shown in Fig. 2(d). Based on peak fitting analysis, the Pd spectrum on the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode shows a pair of spin orbitals. The peaks at 335.0 eV for Pd0 3d5/2 and 340.2 eV for Pd0 3d3/2 indicate that the Pd particles are loaded on the electrode surface in the form of metallic palladium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test and analysis of electrochemical performance of composite electrode
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the electrochemical performance of titanium electrodes, including CQDs-CNTs/Ti supported cathodes and Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti composite cathodes, their charge transfer resistances were first determined by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The results are shown in Fig. 3 (a). The charge transfer resistance (Rct) measured in the low-frequency region indicates the electrocatalytic kinetics at the interface between the electrocatalyst and the electrolyte. This parameter is essential for comprehending the efficiency of the electrochemical reactions taking place at this interface. A smaller radius of the arc in its curve indicates a lower Rct for the electrode and better conductivity [36]. From the figure, it is clear that the arc radius of the electrode after loading Pd and CQDs-CNTs is the smallest, with the impedance radius in the order of Ti matrix > CQDs-CNTs/Ti supported electrode > Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti composite electrode. The Tafel curves for the three electrodes in a 0.5 mol/L Na2SO4 solution, scanned at 0.167 mV/s, are depicted in Fig. 3(b). Under the same conditions, the higher the electrode's hydrogen evolution potential, the higher the hydrogen evolution activity and the more excellent electrochemical performance it usually has. The results in Fig. 3(b) show that both the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti and CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrodes exhibit higher hydrogen precipitation potentials at a certain current density. Moreover, the composite electrode demonstrated the highest hydrogen precipitation potential, indicating that the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode possesses the best electrocatalytic performance and the highest electrocatalytic activity within the electrocatalytic system.

Subsequently, to evaluate the hydrogen precipitation ability of the three electrodes, linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) was conducted without the addition of OTC, using Na2SO4 as the electrolyte solution. The results are shown in Fig. 3(c). Among them, the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode reached a peak current of about 45.4 mA at a potential of 1.4 V, which was 2.05 times higher than that of the CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode and 3.46 times higher than that of the Ti electrode. Additionally, it was observed that the initial potential of the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode shifted in a more positive direction, exhibiting the largest change, followed by the CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode. This result suggests that the loading of Pd NPs and CQDs-CNTs heterojunctions can lead to the generation of more H on the electrode surface. Finally, cyclic voltammetry was used to test the three electrodes to analyze the change in electrochemically active area after loading Pd NPs and CQDs-CNTs. The results are shown in Fig. 3(d). From the figure, it is evident that the electrochemically active area gradually increased after the addition of CQDs-CNTs and Pd NPs compared to the pure Ti electrode. Moreover, compared with the other two electrodes, the CV curve of the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode has the largest encircled area, indicating that it has the largest electrochemically active area. This result again demonstrates that with the addition of Pd NPs and CQDs-CNTs, both can regulate the electron density on the electrode surface, thus enhancing the catalytic performance of the electrocatalytic system [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of composite electrode preparation conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The synergistic interaction between CQDs and CNTs enhances electron transfer, thereby improving electrocatalytic activity. To study the impact of varying ratios of CQDs to CNTs on electrode performance, composite samples with ratios of 1:0.1, 1:0.25, 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:1.5 were prepared and applied to the electrode surface. The findings are presented in Fig. 4 (a). It was noted that, with a constant concentration of CQDs, the degradation efficiency initially increases and then decreases as the concentration of CNTs rises. The maximum degradation efficiency, which was 66.89 %, was obtained when the CQDs:CNTs ratio was set to 1:0.5. This improvement can be explained by the fact that an appropriate increase in the concentration of CNTs boosts the electrode's overall conductivity and facilitates electron transfer between the electrode surface and hygromycin molecules, thereby enhancing degradation efficiency. However, CQDs provide numerous active sites crucial for electrocatalytic reactions. When the CNTs concentration is too high, it may cover or obscure some of these active sites on the CQDs surface. Additionally, the high specific surface area of CNTs provides more adsorption sites, facilitating contact between OTC molecules and catalysts. Nevertheless, a high concentration of CNTs can lead to their agglomeration, reducing the effective surface area and thereby decreasing catalytic efficiency. As illustrated in Fig. 4(b) demonstrates the reaction kinetics of the supported electrodes prepared at different ratios of CQDs-CNTs during the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC. The figure shows that the process conforms to first-order reaction kinetics, indicating a consistent rate of degradation over time. When the CQDs:CNTs ratio is 1:0.5, the k-value reaches its maximum (0.0195 min−1), signifying the highest reaction rate. Consequently, the optimal CQDs:CNTs ratio is established as 1:0.5.

After identifying the optimal ratio of CODs and CNTs, we examined how varying the concentration of CQDs-CNTs impacted electrode performance. The findings are presented in Fig. 4(c). As the concentration of the CQDs-CNTs solution rose from 0.15 to 3.00 mg/L, the degradation efficiencies for hygromycin hydrochloride were recorded as 62.9 %, 66.9 %, 69.0 %, 67.5 %, and 67.1 % respectively (Fig. 4(d)). The corresponding reaction rate constant k values were 0.0171 min−1, 0.0195 min−1, 0.0213 min−1, 0.0199 min−1, and 0.0194 min−1. These results indicate that the degradation effect initially increases with the concentration of CQDs-CNTs but then decreases at higher concentrations. This is because the catalytic activity of the electrode is improved with the addition of CQDs-CNTs. The enhanced performance can be attributed to several factors. Firstly, CQDs provide additional functional groups and defects that serve as active sites for catalysis. Secondly, CNTs offer excellent electrical conductivity, facilitating efficient electron transfer between the electrode and the electrolyte. However, excessive loading can cause shedding of active substances from the electrode surface, leading to a reduced degradation effect. By combining the reaction rate constant and degradation efficiency, we determined that the optimal CQDs-CNTs deposition solution concentration is 1.50 mg/L.

Finally, the effect of Pd nanoparticle content on the performance of Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrodes was investigated. The loading of Pd NPs on the electrode surface was varied by adjusting the concentration of PdCl2 solution in the deposition solution. The optimal Pd deposition concentration was determined based on the degradation rates of OTC at different electrodes. Fig. 4(e) shows that the degradation efficiencies of OTC were 69.0 %, 71.8 %, 71.9 %, 73.2 %, 71.3 %, and 71.3 % when the concentration of the deposition solution ranged from 0 to 25 μmol/L. Additionally, Fig. 4(f) indicates that the degradation rate of OTC initially increased and then decreased as the loading of Pd NPs increased. This trend suggests that higher Pd content leads to nanoparticle agglomeration and potential shedding of the active layer [38]. Moreover, Fig. 4(f) reveals that the reaction rate constant gradually increased with increasing Pd content, from an initial value of 0.0213 min−1 to 0.0239 min−1. Considering all these factors, the optimum concentration of deposition solution was chosen to be 15 μmol/L.

After optimizing the ratio of CODs to CNTs, the concentration of CQDs-CNTs, and the concentration of PdCl2, the optimal conditions for synthesizing Pd/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrodes were established. Subsequently, the degradation efficiencies of Ti electrodes, CNTs/Ti electrodes, CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrodes, and Pd/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrodes were compared under these optimized conditions. As illustrated in Fig. S3, the initial degradation performance of the Ti electrode stood at merely 54.39 %. With the incorporation of CNTs, a notable enhancement in degradation efficiency was observed, recording a 6.36 % increase. This improvement can be partially attributed to the superior electronic conductivity of CNTs, which facilitates rapid electron transfer. Specifically, in CNTs/Ti composite electrodes, CNTs serve as efficient conduits for electrons, expediting their journey to reactive sites and thereby accelerating the degradation of OTC. The subsequent introduction of CQDs into the CNT framework led to an additional boost in degradation efficiency for CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrodes, with an 8.28 % increase. This enhancement stems from the augmented number of reactive sites on the electrode's surface due to the inclusion of CQDs. Their diminutive size and expansive specific surface area enable CQDs to offer more adsorption and reaction locales for OTC molecules, all while preserving the electrode's overall structure. Moreover, a potent synergistic interaction exists between CQDs and CNTs, where CQDs, by anchoring onto CNT surfaces, further facilitate expeditious electron transfer. Lastly, the integration of Pd NPs into the CQDs-CNTs/Ti matrix yielded yet another leap in electrode performance. This heightened efficiency is twofold: Pd NPs function as co-catalysts, lowering the activation energy required for bond cleavage in OTC molecules, and their unique crystalline and electronic structures generate a synergistic effect when combined with CQDs, CNTs, and Ti. The presence of Pd NPs modulates the electronic architecture of the entire composite electrode, enhancing its affinity for OTC molecule adsorption and degradation. Furthermore, it aids in the diffusion of reaction products away from the electrode surface, mitigating product accumulation that could otherwise impede reaction progress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of experimental conditions on electrocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: After identifying the optimal electrode preparation conditions, we examined various factors affecting the degradation process. Given that NaCl is a crucial component of seawater, its concentration plays a significant role in electrode performance. Consequently, this study investigated how the electrode performs in solutions with different NaCl concentrations to verify its effectiveness in high-salinity environments. The effects of pH 4.5 and NaCl concentration of 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.30, 0.40, 0.50 and 0.60 mol/L on the degradation were investigated. From Fig. 5 (a), it can be seen that the change of NaCl concentration in the experimental range had a large effect on the OTC degradation efficiency. The degradation rate of the OTC rose progressively with increasing NaCl concentration. This observation is highly encouraging, suggesting that the electrode can exhibit superior performance in seawater with high salt content. When the concentration of NaCl reached 0.60 mol/L, the degradation efficiency was as high as 78.19 %. Consequently, a NaCl concentration of 0.60 mol/L was selected as the electrolyte concentration for the degradation experiment, which corresponds to the average NaCl concentration found in seawater.

As the main source of energy in the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC, the current can have a significant impact on the electrochemical degradation process. In addition, the current magnitude is also related to the production of oxidizing active species in the system. To investigate the effect of current on OTC degradation, degradation experiments were performed at a NaCl concentration of 0.6 mol/L and a pH of 4.5 with currents of 0, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, and 13 mA, as shown in Fig. 5 (b). The degradation efficiency showed a positive correlation with the current intensity, rising as the current increased. When the current intensity reached 11 mA, the degradation efficiency was 89.47 %. Further increasing the current to 13 mA resulted in a degradation efficiency of 90.43 %, showing only a small enhancement. As depicted in Fig. 5(c), the reaction rate constants were 0.0147, 0.0216, 0.0230, 0.0255, 0.0327, and 0.0342 min−1 for currents ranging from 1 to 13 mA, respectively. The trend of the reaction rate constants aligned with the degradation efficiency. This was due to the fact that too low a current intensity limited the amount of electron transfer between the electrodes and the pollutants, as well as the electron transfer rate, resulting in a lower degradation effect. On the other hand, an excessively high current would trigger side reactions, thereby decreasing current efficiency and increasing energy consumption. Thus, taking energy consumption into account, 11 mA was chosen as the optimal degradation current. In addition, this experiment explored the changes in OTC degradation rate by the composite electrode when the environmental pH value was changed. As shown in Fig. 5(d), the degradation efficiencies were 75.4 %, 82.5 %, 89.5 %, 89.2 %, 89.1 %, 87.3 %, and 79.1 % when the ambient pH values were 1, 3, 4.5, 5, 7, 9, and 11, respectively. As the environmental pH gradually increased, the removal rate of OTC by the electrocatalytic system showed a tendency to increase first and then decrease. The peak degradation rate (89.47 %) was reached when the environmental pH was 4.5. When the pH is too low, the corrosion of Pd NPs on the electrode surface occurs. These corroded Pd NPs then interact with the catalyst surface, forming compounds with no catalytic activity. Additionally, the structures of CNTs and CQDs are destroyed under acidic conditions. This leads to a decrease in the activity of the catalyst and a reduction in degradation efficiency. On the other hand, at high pH levels, the generation of ·OH was hindered. Notably, the degradation efficiency of hygromycin hydrochloride reached approximately 80 % when the pH ranged from 3 to 11, demonstrating that the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrodes are highly adaptable to various pH conditions and exhibit superior degradation effects across a wide pH range. Fig. 5(e) illustrates the determination of the zero charge point (pHpzc) of the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode. The pHpzc of this electrode is 5.44. Combining this with the molecular morphology of OTC molecules in different pH solutions discussed in chapter 3.4.7, it is evident that the theoretical predictions align well with the practical results. The electrode demonstrates excellent degradation performance between pH 3.57 and 7.49.

OTC solutions with initial concentrations of 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 20, 40, and 60 mg/L were prepared, and electrochemical degradation experiments were conducted sequentially. The results were also aligned with a kinetic equation, as illustrated in Fig. 5(f). The figure indicates that the degradation efficiency of solutions with varying initial concentrations of OTC decreases as the OTC concentration rises. Additionally, the reaction rate constant initially increases and then decreases with an increase in OTC concentration. At an OTC concentration of 20 mg/L, both the degradation efficiency and the reaction rate constant achieved their peak values, reaching 89.5 % and 0.0327 min−1, respectively (Fig. 5g). This is because high OTC concentrations lead to a large accumulation of OTC molecules on the anode surface, which competes with H* for the catalyst's active sites. Excessive OTC adsorbed on the electrode surface hinders the generation of H*. Additionally, the production of other intermediate products increases, affecting the contact between the active site and OTC molecules, thereby reducing the degradation efficiency. Thus, the optimal initial concentration of OTC was determined to be 20 mg/L. Temperature plays a crucial role in the electrocatalytic process. Therefore, it is essential to investigate the impact of reaction temperature on the degradation efficiency of OTC. The reaction temperatures were set at 20℃, 25℃, 30℃, 35℃, and 40℃, with the results depicted in Fig. 5(h). The degradation efficiencies were recorded as 90.4 %, 90.3 %, 89.6 %, 89.5 %, and 87.9 % for temperatures ranging from 20℃ to 40℃. As the temperature increased from 20℃ to 40℃, the degradation efficiency of OTC gradually decreased. This trend can be explained by the reduction in solution conductivity with rising temperature. Additionally, elevated temperatures increase molecular collisions, thereby speeding up side reactions and decreasing current efficiency and ·OH utilization. However, within the 60-minute reaction period, the variation in reaction temperature had minimal impact on the degradation of OTC, with all degradation efficiencies around 88 %. Considering energy consumption, the optimum reaction temperature for this reaction system is at room temperature, specifically 25 °C.

To conclusively demonstrate the exceptional performance of the electrode in this study, we compared the efficacy of the Pd/CQDs-CNTs/Ti composite electrode against other composite electrodes, with the results summarized in Table S1. Notably, the Pd/CQDs-CNTs/Ti composite electrode exhibits not only high oxytetracycline (OTC) degradation efficiency but also maintains optimal performance at relatively mild pH levels, showcasing advantages across a broad pH spectrum. Consequently, this composite electrode presents significant research value and substantial application potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and generalizability analysis of composite electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the stability of the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti composite electrode, we examined its lifespan. Fig. 6 (a) displays the experimental outcomes of ten cycles of electrocatalytic OTC degradation. The electrode's stability was indirectly measured by the OTC removal rate after each cycle. The findings suggest that the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode maintained a consistent activity, with a removal rate of approximately 90 %. This highlights the excellent stability of the prepared Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti composite electrode.

To further assess the efficacy of the electrocatalytic approach, we compared the degradation of OTC using the Pd/CQDs-CNTs/Ti composite electrode with other established techniques, including ozonation via MNB, photocatalysis, biosorption, and PAA activation. The results, summarized in Table S2, clearly illustrate that electrocatalysis outperforms these methods in terms of degradation time and efficiency. Notably, it achieves higher degradation rates than both biosorption and PAA activation. Consequently, the Pd/CQDs-CNTs/Ti composite electrode emerges as an efficient and environmentally friendly solution for OTC degradation.

To assess the practicality of the fabricated Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrodes, we examined their performance using various antibiotics. Specifically, we chose tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), doxycycline hydrochloride (DOX), norfloxacin (NOR), sulfamethoxazole (SMZ), and tylosin (TYL) as target pollutants for electrochemical oxidative degradation experiments. Each pollutant was initially present at a concentration of 20 mg/L, with a current intensity set to 11 mA, an electrolyte concentration of 0.50 mol/L, and a reaction temperature maintained at 25 ℃. After 60 min of electrolysis, the removal efficiency for each pollutant was illustrated in Fig. 6(b). Among these pollutants, the composite electrode exhibited a slightly higher removal efficiency for TC (90.6 %) compared to OTC (90.3 %). This can be attributed to the chemical structure of tetracycline, which contains multiple aromatic rings and functional groups, making it more susceptible to oxidation during the electrocatalytic process. Additionally, TC molecules are more likely to be adsorbed onto the surface of CNTs than OTC molecules, increasing their degradation probability. Furthermore, the composite electrodes achieved over 60 % degradation rate for all other antibiotics, with degradation efficiencies reaching 73.9 %, 71.7 %, 64.9 %, and 63.5 % for DOX, NOR, SMZ, and TYL, respectively. This is because the functional groups and aromatic ring structures in these antibiotic molecules are more stable than those in hygromycin hydrochloride, making them harder to oxidize during the electrocatalytic process. Moreover, their functional group arrangement and spatial configurations lead to lower reactivity on the surface of the electrocatalysts, thus reducing degradation efficiency. Nevertheless, the composite electrodes exhibited effective degradation across various antibiotics, suggesting that the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti composite electrodes developed in this research have wide applicability in antibiotic degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance testing of composite electrodes in actual seawater
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the efficacy of Pd/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrodes in real-world marine aquaculture wastewater, an electrocatalytic degradation experiment was conducted using water from a marine aquaculture facility in the Yellow Sea. The test compared the degradation efficiency of OTC by the composite electrode in both simulated seawater and actual seawater, with results illustrated in Fig. S4(a). Notably, the composite electrode achieved a degradation efficiency of 91.36 % for OTC in real seawater within 60 min, surpassing its performance in simulated seawater by 1.05 %. This enhancement is attributed to the presence of additional ions like Mg2+, Ca2+, and SO4 2- in real seawater, beyond Cl-. These ions engage in complex interactions with the electrode surface, altering charge distribution and the chemical milieu, which in turn fosters the degradation process and boosts efficiency. Furthermore, the actual seawater contains diverse organic substances and microorganisms that interact synergistically with OTC, facilitating its degradation through mechanisms such as serving as electron shuttles. Microbial metabolic activities also play a pivotal role, modifying the electrode's surrounding chemical environment—through localized pH shifts or secretion of catalytically active compounds—thereby further augmenting OTC's degradation efficiency.

To further assess the stability of the composite electrode in real-world conditions, it underwent ten cyclic tests in actual seawater, with the findings presented in Fig. S4(b). The data reveals that the Pd/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode maintained its integrity throughout these cycles, with degradation efficiency consistently exceeding 91 %. This robust performance underscores the composite electrode's remarkable electrochemical stability in seawater and its resilience against erosive forces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of reaction mechanisms and identification of active species
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the generation of ·OH during the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC, coumarin was employed for fluorescence spectroscopy analysis. The concentration of ·OH was indirectly indicated by the fluorescence intensity of 7-hydroxycoumarin (7-HC) produced after coumarin captured ·OH [39,40]. As illustrated in Fig. 7 (a), the fluorescence intensity of 7-HC increases progressively with the extension of degradation time. This observation confirms the presence of ·OH during the electrocatalytic reaction and suggests that its concentration rises with reaction time. Additionally, the formation of ·OH at the anode is typically accompanied by the production of H* at the cathode. To further validate the existence of ·OH and H, quenching agents were introduced to detect these reactive species. IPA (isopropanol) is commonly used as a quenching agent for ·OH, while TBA (tert-butyl alcohol) quenches both ·OH and H* [41]. As illustrated in Fig. 7(b), the addition of 1M IPA led to a 6.63 % decrease in the degradation rate of OTC by the composite electrode, confirming the presence of ·OH during the degradation process and its role in enhancing the degradation of OTC. When 1M TBA was added, the degradation efficiency dropped by 9.14 %, which was lower than when only IPA was added, indicating the production of H* at the cathode surface. In summary, the results of the quenching experiments show that ·OH and H* are present during the electrocatalytic process, and together they contribute to the improvement of OTC degradation efficiency. Additionally, to precisely detect the generation of ·OH during the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC by the composite electrode, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was employed. The results are shown in Fig. S5. As illustrated in the figure, an evident characteristic peak with a ratio of 1:2:2:1 for ·OH was detected, confirming the presence of ·OH throughout the electrocatalytic degradation process.

Due to the high concentration of NaCl in natural seawater, practical applications reveal that the substantial amount of Cl- in seawater facilitates the formation of active chlorine radicals on the anode surface [42]. These chlorine radicals, generated by oxidation on the anode surface, react with OTC, thereby enhancing the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC. Fig. 7(c) demonstrates that linear voltammetry scanning in the NaCl system can confirm the presence of active chlorine species. In the absence of NaCl, the LSV scan of the anode only shows an oxygen evolution reaction, with an oxygen evolution potential of 1.25 V. As the concentration of Cl- increases, the response current at 1.25 V also gradually rises. This occurs because, when Cl- is present, the substance undergoing oxidation on the anode surface shifts from H2O to Cl- (Eq. (1)), and since Cl- is more readily oxidized, the chlorine evolution potential is typically lower than the oxygen evolution potential [43]. Additionally, as the concentration of Cl- increases, the chlorine evolution potential also gradually increases, resulting in a gradual increase in response current. Thus, this outcome suggests the presence of active chlorine entities during the electrocatalytic degradation process. It is important to note that in acidic conditions, active chlorine species are present as HClO (Eq. (2)). In contrast, under alkaline conditions, the highly oxidative HClO reacts with free OH– ions in water to form the less oxidative ClO- (Eq. (3)). Since the actual pH value of seawater is slightly alkaline, both HClO and ClO- will coexist, with ClO- being the predominant form.

(3) HClO + OH–→ClO-+H2O

In the electrocatalytic process, the H2O2 generated on the cathode surface plays a very important role [44]. In this study, we detected the H2O2 generated during the reaction process using the iodometric method. This method allows us to analyze the concentration changes of H2O2 produced on the cathode surface after the start of the electrocatalytic degradation reaction. As illustrated in Fig. 7(d), the findings demonstrate that as the degradation time extends, the concentration of H2O2 progressively rises, attaining a level of 0.235 mmol/L after 60 min of degradation. These results imply that H2O2 can be produced on the cathode surface via the oxygen reduction process during the degradation (Eq. (4)). When H2O2 is produced on the cathode surface, it is further reduced to OH– and ·OH in the electron-rich environment on the cathode surface (Eq. (5)). Consequently, the generated ·OH contributes partially to the degradation of OTC. Thus, the H2O2 produced on the cathode surface assists in the breakdown of OTC (Fig. 7e, steps ⑥ and ⑦).

In the cathode–anode synergistic system, the degradation mechanism is primarily divided into two processes: oxidation at the anode and reduction at the cathode. Direct and indirect oxidation occurs at the anode; direct oxidation involves the direct oxidation of OTC molecules at the anode surface via electron transfer (Eq. (6)). In contrast, indirect oxidation occurs due to the conversion of water molecules or other anions near the anode into non-homogeneous hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and other reactive oxygen species in an electron-deficient environment near the anode, followed by the release of electrons (Eq. (7)). Consequently, OTC molecules near the anode undergo oxidative reactions with these radicals, which are physically adsorbed onto the anode surface, thereby degrading into small molecules (Eq. (8)). On the other hand, the reaction mechanism diagram in Fig. 7e illustrates that the reduction reactions occurring at the cathode are mainly categorized into direct and indirect reduction. Direct electroreduction occurs due to the presence of an applied electric field. When a hygromycin molecule moves near the cathode, it is directly reduced by the electrode (Fig. 7e, step ③). Meanwhile, indirect reduction begins with the generation of free radicals on the cathode surface, which then participate in the electrocatalytic reduction reaction. Since an electron-rich environment is formed on the cathode surface, a large amount of hydrated hydrogen ions (H3O+) exists near the cathode due to the acidic environment. These H3O+ react with electrons to generate a large number of activated hydrogen atoms (H*) in the adsorbed state through the Volmer pathway (Eq. (9), steps ① and ②). In this process, the titanium substrate of this composite cathode provides a stable support structure for the composite catalyst, ensuring its overall stability and durability [45]. Additionally, the substrate's excellent electrical conductivity serves as a transport channel for electrons, promoting rapid electron transfer within the catalyst. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) further enhance this by accelerating electron transfer due to their own superior electrical conductivity [46]. Meanwhile, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) act as electron donors or acceptors [47], forming effective electron transfer channels with palladium (Pd) nanoparticles, thereby improving the overall electron transfer efficiency. This efficient electron transfer boosts both the rate and efficiency of the catalytic reaction. The Pd nanoparticles, being the main catalytic activity center, contribute significantly to the degradation efficiency of the catalyst for OTC due to their high specific surface area and abundant active sites [48]. The addition of CNTs and CQDs increases the specific surface area of the composite electrode and provides more active sites, enhancing the catalytic activity. Furthermore, the network structure of CNTs facilitates mass transfer of reactants and products, accelerating the diffusion of reactants to the catalytic active center and the removal of products from it. The excellent mechanical properties and chemical stability of CNTs protect the Pd nanoparticles from external environmental erosion, while CQDs prevent the agglomeration of Pd nanoparticles by forming stable interfacial interactions with them [49]. Notably, when CQDs are anchored inside carbon nanotubes, a heterojunction structure is formed due to differences in structure and properties between the two. This structure improves the electron transport and surface properties of the catalyst, further enhancing its activity and stability. As a result, the interaction between components on the surface of the composite electrode creates a large number of active sites that help to activate hydrogen atom generation. However, since generating H2 requires a more negative potential than producing H*, H* quickly converts into H2 via the Heyrovsky and Tafel pathways (Eq. (10)–(11), steps ④ and ⑤), a process known as the hydrogen precipitation side reaction. Consequently, bubbles form on the cathode surface during operation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LC-MS analysis and analysis of OTC mineralization pathways
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the reaction mechanism of OTC, the intermediate products were analyzed by LC-MS. Under optimal conditions, the prepared Pd/CQDs-CNTs/Ti composite electrode was employed to electrocatalytic the degradation of OTC. Water samples were collected every 10 min during the reaction for analysis. The molecular structures of the final possible intermediate products are presented in Table S3. Based on the degradation intermediates of OTC, the degradation pathway of OTC was introduced, as shown in Fig. 8 . The degradation of OTC primarily involves processes such as deamination, hydroxylation, dealkylation, and dehydration. Reactive substances preferentially target electron-donating and ionizable groups, leading to the direct destruction of double bonds, amide groups, amino groups, etc. As the reaction progresses, the benzene ring is also degraded. In this process, ·OH plays a key role in the oxidation of OTC, while H* is mainly responsible for its reduction. Additionally, HClO contributes to the ring-opening cleavage of OTC. The specific pathways are divided into four: (1) OTC (m/z = 461) undergoes a process where the dimethylamino group is removed, resulting in the formation of P1 (m/z = 418). Subsequently, P1 undergoes ring-opening, dehydration, and protonation, leading to the gradual formation of P2 (m/z = 303), P3 (m/z = 139), and P4 (m/z = 115). (2) The OTC molecule undergoes the removal of two methyl groups, resulting in the formation of P5 (m/z = 433). P5 then undergoes deamination, dehydration, and ring-opening reactions, leading to the formation of P6 (m/z = 359) and P7 (m/z = 261). Subsequently, P7 undergoes carbon–carbon single bond breakage and hydroxylation, resulting in the formation of P8 (m/z = 195). (3) OTC underwent dehydration to give P9 (m/z = 429). P9 underwent ring opening and removal of a water molecule to give P11 (m/z = 259). (4) OTC removes two methyl groups in dimethylamino group and dehydration to obtain P10 (m/z = 401), and further ring-opening to obtain P11. P4, P8 and P11 can be gradually degraded to P12 (m/z = 109), P13 (m/z = 103) and P14 (m/z = 89) small molecule organic products. Finally these small molecule products can be mineralized into water and carbon dioxide as well as other inorganic ions.

The toxicity of OTC degradation intermediates in electrocatalytically treated wastewater was assessed using the Ecological Structure Activity Relationship (ECOSAR) procedure. The toxicity levels for target aquatic organisms, including fish, daphnia, and green algae, were classified according to the Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labeling of Chemicals (GHS) into four categories: non-hazardous, hazardous, toxic, and highly toxic. Fig. 9 (a-c) illustrates acute toxicity, while Fig. 9(d-e) presents chronic toxicity data, the specific values of its toxicity are shown in Table S4. These figures reveal that P2 and P11 exhibit higher acute and chronic toxicity compared to OTC molecules. However, P2 and P11 can be further converted into less toxic products such as P3 and P12. Additionally, all other intermediates demonstrate comparable or even lower toxicity than OTC. Consequently, the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC by Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrodes is confirmed as a green and safe process technology, ensuring minimal impact on aquatic organisms and the safety of the effluent water quality.

Subsequently, the quantitative conformational relationship prediction of bioaccumulation factor, mutagenicity, and developmental toxicity for OTC and its intermediates was conducted using Toxicity Evaluation Software (TEST), with results presented in Fig. 9g-i and Table S5. As shown in Fig. 9(g), most intermediates exhibited reduced bioaccumulation factors compared to OTC. However, P2, P4, P7, P11, and P12 had higher bioaccumulation factors than OTC. Nevertheless, these five products could be further converted to less bioaccumulative substances such as P3, P8, P13, and P14 as the reaction progressed. Regarding developmental toxicity, OTC was classified as a “developmental toxicant,” while most degradation intermediates showed reduced developmental toxicity compared to the original OTC molecules, as illustrated in Fig. 9(h). Furthermore, the mutagenic potential of OTC and its degradation byproducts was assessed. As shown in Fig. 9(i), OTC was classified as “ortho-mutagenic,” meaning it exhibited positive mutagenicity. Conversely, most of the intermediates had lower mutagenicity levels compared to OTC, with five products being labeled as “negatively mutagenic,” indicating they were non-mutagenic. These results suggest that the electrocatalytic treatment using the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode significantly diminishes both the toxicity and mutagenicity of OTC.

In practical applications, energy consumption is a critical factor in assessing the application potential of electrocatalytic systems. To more precisely assess the potential for applying Pd NPs/CNTs-CQDs/Ti electrodes in the electrocatalytic degradation of OTC, this study determined the energy consumption per unit volume (EEO: kWh/m3). The EEO was calculated using Eq. (12) as shown below.
(12)
E EO
=
E cell
It
1000 × V × log(
C 0
/
C t
)
.

The electrolysis cell voltage is denoted as Ecell (V), the current as I (A), the treatment time as t (h), and the volume of treated water as V (m3). The initial and final concentrations of OTC are represented by C0 and Ct (mg/L), respectively. This study found that the Pd NPs/CNTs-CQDs/Ti electrode achieved a degradation efficiency of 90.3 % for OTC, with an energy consumption of 0.366 kWh/m3. This demonstrates that the Pd NPs/CNTs-CQDs/Ti electrode can degrade OTC with minimal energy usage, resulting in lower energy costs. As a result, the electrode is shown to be an effective and energy-efficient choice for degrading OTC.

After calculation, the energy consumption for OTC degradation in the current study was compared with the energy consumption values of other documented contaminants, including doxycycline hydrochloride (DOX), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), levofloxacin (LFXN) and nitrobenzene (NB), as detailed in Table S6. The results of the study show that only 0.366 kWh/m3 of energy is required to degrade 90.31 % of OTC, which is significantly lower compared to other literature reports. The results show that the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti composite electrode not only has excellent OTC degradation efficiency, but also has very low energy consumption, which is a particularly outstanding advantage in practical applications, resulting in a significant reduction in actual operating costs.

In this study, CQDs were prepared by electrochemical etching and encapsulated into CNTs through heating, stirring, and sonication. Subsequently, Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrodes were fabricated by depositing CQDs-CNTs and Pd particles via two constant current depositions. These electrodes were then utilized for the electrocatalytic degradation of hygromycin hydrochloride in seawater. TEM results confirmed that the CQDs were successfully encapsulated inside the CNTs, forming highly conjugated CQDs-CNTs heterojunctions. The attachment of CQDs exposed more active sites on the electrode surface. Electrochemical performance tests confirmed the successful incorporation of CQDs-CNTs and Pd onto the Ti electrode. The addition of Pd helped to adjust the electron density on the electrode surface, greatly enhancing its catalytic performance. The Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode showed the lowest charge-transfer resistance and the highest electrochemical activity, along with a high capability for producing H* radicals. A series of electrocatalytic degradation experiments were conducted to determine the optimal preparation conditions for the electrode and the experimental parameters. After ten consecutive degradation cycles, the efficiency remained largely unchanged at about 90 %. Free radical trapping experiments, LSV, and hydrogen peroxide concentration determination revealed that ·OH, H*, and H2O2 were the main active species in the electrocatalytic system. Additionally, the conversion of Cl- to active chlorine species in seawater was found to play a significant role in OTC degradation. The synergistic action between the cathode and anode was proposed to co-catalyze the degradation of OTC, enhancing its degradation rate. Possible degradation intermediates of OTC were hypothesized based on LC-MS analysis. Toxicity assessments using TEST and ECOSAR 2.2 indicated that OTC molecules were degraded into low-toxicity small molecules, which were ultimately mineralized into CO2 and H2O. This ensured minimal impact on aquatic organisms and guaranteed the safety of the effluent water quality. Moreover, in terms of energy consumption, the Pd NPs/CQDs-CNTs/Ti electrode degraded OTC with an energy consumption of only 0.366 kWh/m3. In summary, the composite electrode can efficiently and stably degrade OTC under high-salt conditions. Its green and low-consumption features further illustrate its potential application in the electrocatalytic degradation of antibiotic wastewater in seawater. This provides new insights for managing high concentrations of antibiotics in mariculture wastewater.
